channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Trump Embraces U.S. Military Power After Years of Caution
item.description: The wars in Afghanistan and Iraq offered a stark lesson in the limits of military force. The Iran attacks suggest an era of postwar wariness is over.
item.dc:creator: Greg Jaffe
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump in Corpus Christi, Texas, last week. His sudden embrace of U.S. military power is a stunning reversal.

item.title: ‘Pray We Make It Through the Night’: Iran’s Capital Under Siege
item.description: Many residents of Tehran managed to get out of town when the U.S. and Israel attack began, but others who could not described living under bombardment.
item.dc:creator: Farnaz Fassihi and Arash Khamooshi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Destruction of Cultural Antiquities and Heritage
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: People are standing behind a yellow line after a police station on Niloufar street was hit by US-Israel attacks and damaged the next door buildings in Tehran on Monday.

item.title: U.S. Sends More Troops to the Mideast as Iran War Expands
item.description: Gen. Dan Caine, the chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, acknowledged the possibility of an extended campaign, as the military announced that six U.S. service members had been killed so far.
item.dc:creator: Helene Cooper and Eric Schmitt
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and Gen. Dan Caine, the chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, during the Trump administration’s first news conference on the war in Iran, on Monday.

item.title: Texas Senate Candidates Make Final Arguments Before Primary Elections
item.description: The Republicans John Cornyn and Ken Paxton — and the Democrats Jasmine Crockett and James Talarico — are battling in bitter and expensive races.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Medina
item.media:credit: Jordan Vonderhaar for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator John Cornyn greeted by supporters at a campaign event in Schertz, Texas, on Monday.

item.title: In Republican Win, Supreme Court Retains G.O.P. District in New York
item.description: In an emergency ruling, the justices preserved the district of a Republican congresswoman, despite a lower-court ruling that it illegally diluted the power of minority voters.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle and Grace Ashford
item.media:credit: Adam Gray for The New York Times
item.media:description: A voter at a polling center on Staten Island in October.

item.title: Can These Democrats Make Combative Centrism Happen for 2028 Election?
item.description: Third Way, the moderate Democratic advocacy group and think tank, has an expansive and expensive plan to shape the 2028 presidential campaign.
item.dc:creator: Katie Glueck
item.media:description: Jon Cowan, left, president of the group Third Way, and Jim Messina, a Democratic strategist.

item.title: Trump Administration Abandons Efforts to Impose Executive Orders on Law Firms
item.description: The move amounts to a surrender in a clash that has led many law firms to submit to the president rather than face the threat of his executive orders.
item.dc:creator: Jonah E. Bromwich and Michael S. Schmidt
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Justice Department told the U.S. Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia on Monday that it was no longer interested in pursuing the cases contesting executive orders barring law firms from government business.

item.title: OpenAI Amends A.I. Deal With the Pentagon
item.description: The new pact includes additional protections to prevent the use of the company’s technology for mass surveillance of Americans.
item.dc:creator: Cade Metz and Julian E. Barnes
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: “It’s critical to protect the civil liberties of Americans, and there was so much focus on this, that we wanted to make this point especially clear,” OpenAI’s chief executive, Sam Altman, center, wrote on social media.

item.title: What Is Trump’s Neck Rash? His Doctor Declines to Explain.
item.description: President Trump’s physician did not explain what the skin condition was or what medication he was using to treat it.
item.dc:creator: Chris Cameron
item.media:description: President Trump at the White House on Monday.

item.title: Trump Says He Will End Boycott of White House Correspondents’ Dinner
item.description: The mentalist Oz Pearlman is this year’s speaker. “Hopefully, it will be something very Special,” the president wrote on Truth Social.
item.dc:creator: Shawn McCreesh and Michael M. Grynbaum
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Marine Corps Band playing at the White House Correspondents’ Dinner in Washington in 2024.

item.title: Supreme Court Sides With Religious Parents, Blocking California’s Trans Student Policy
item.description: Christian teachers and parents challenged the state’s policies, which they say require schools to hide students’ transgender status from their parents.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: A protester holding a transgender flag outside the Supreme Court last month.

item.title: N.Y. Attorney General Orders Hospital to Resume Youth Transgender Care
item.description: NYU Langone Health had stopped providing puberty-blocking medication and hormone treatments after the federal government threatened to pull its funding.
item.dc:creator: Joseph Goldstein
item.category: New York University Langone Medical Center
item.media:description: Demonstrators rallied at NYU Langone Health in February 2025 demanding that the hospital commit to providing services to transgender adolescents.

item.title: As Kennedy Turns From Vaccines, MAHA Allies See a ‘Betrayal’
item.description: As the midterm elections approach, the health secretary is focusing on “real food.” Anger is rising among some of his supporters.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mueller
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:credit: Callaghan O'Hare for The New York Times
item.media:description: Derrick Wynne was discharged from the Army for refusing a Covid shot. “He was using our movement to gain a huge amount of traction,” he said of Robert F. Kennedy, Jr.

item.title: States Move to Limit Access to H.I.V. Treatment
item.description: Citing rising costs and shortfalls in federal support, about 20 states are toughening eligibility requirements for patients in drug assistance programs.
item.dc:creator: Apoorva Mandavilli
item.media:description: This week, thousands of Floridians were cut off from benefits that make H.I.V. medications more affordable. The medication Biktarvy was also dropped from Florida’s formulary.

item.title: America’s Billionaires Continue to Flock to Wyoming
item.description: Jackson, Wyo., has long been a refuge for the rich. But the last five years saw a boom in wealth of a kind never before seen. Across the country, the 2017 tax cuts minted hundreds of new billionaires.
item.dc:creator: Katie Benner, Steven Rich, Mike Baker, John Branch and Will Warasila
item.media:credit: Will Warasila for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jackson, Wyo. Teton County is both the richest county in America and a place that in some areas is struggling to maintain basic services.

item.title: Seven Takeaways From the Clintons’ Epstein Depositions
item.description: Former Secretary of State Hillary Clinton was defiant. Former President Bill Clinton spoke of President Trump’s ties to Epstein. A Republican raised a conspiracy theory.
item.dc:creator: Annie Karni and Michael Gold
item.media:description: Hillary Clinton speaking to reporters after her closed-door deposition before the House Oversight Committee, as part of its investigation into Jeffrey Epstein, in Chappaqua, N.Y.

item.title: As Trump Scrambles the World Order, Can Germany Learn the Language of Hard Power?
item.description: In the wake of the U.S. bombing of Iran and its dismissal of European allies, an anxious continent’s best chance at security runs through its largest economy.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Zerofsky

item.title: When Trump Meets Germany’s Merz, Iran Will Likely Dominate Discussion
item.description: Chancellor Friedrich Merz’s visit to Washington, long planned, is now likely to be dominated by discussions of the attack, which Europeans did not take part in.
item.dc:creator: Jim Tankersley
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Chancellor Friedrich Merz of Germany, left, with President Trump in October.

item.title: Under Pressure from Trump, Cuban Leader Calls for ‘Urgent’ Economic Change
item.description: President Miguel Díaz-Canel said the private sector needed more autonomy, as the island confronts a U.S. oil blockade that has deepened a humanitarian crisis.
item.dc:creator: Luis Ferré-Sadurní and David C. Adams
item.media:description: President Miguel Díaz-Canel of Cuba, center, at a funeral in January for those killed in U.S. strikes on Venezuela.

item.title: When ‘Anora’ Becomes ‘Anorak’: The Art of Wrong Movie Titles
item.description: Staffers at Film at Lincoln Center keep a list of the incorrect movie titles they’ve heard from patrons. That list is very, very long.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Huff and Tala Safie

item.title: The Iranian Revolution That Wasn’t
item.description: Ayatollah Khomeini’s bait-and-switch game in the early years of the Islamic regime turned many of the people against it. They’ve been fighting the revolution since.
item.dc:creator: Abbas Milani

item.title: Americans Need to Know More About The Iran Attacks
item.description: The American public has received too little information to effectively judge the goals and objectives of the largest U.S. military operation in the Middle East in a generation.
item.dc:creator: W.J. Hennigan
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Photo Illustration by Damon Winter/The New York Times

item.title: U.S. Slaps Sanctions on Rwanda, Saying It Sabotaged Trump Peace Deal
item.description: Rwanda’s government responded by claiming the sanctions unjustly targeted only one party to the conflict and misrepresented the facts.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Mpoke Bigg
item.category: War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:description: President Trump, President Paul Kagame of Rwanda, center, and President Felix Tshisekedi of the Democratic Republic of Congo participate in a signing ceremony at the Institute of Peace in Washington in December 2025.

item.title: Your Closest Forest Gets Lit Up by a Hidden Purple Glow
item.description: Research shows that a phenomenon called St. Elmo’s Fire, which occurs during thunderstorms, may be much more common than previously understood.
item.dc:creator: Annie Roth

item.title: Blood Moon Total Lunar Eclipse 2026: Time and Visibility Guide
item.description: Earth’s shadow will temporarily drape the moon in a copper hue before sunrise on Tuesday.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller
item.media:description: To best view the phenomenon, NASA recommends finding a dark place away from bright lights.

item.title: Half of Colorectal Cancer Cases Now Occur in Those Under 65, Study Shows
item.description: It isn’t clear what’s driving the shift, but scientists suspect that environmental exposures may be partially to blame.
item.dc:creator: Nina Agrawal
item.media:credit: Deanna Donegan/The New York Times; Photographs by Getty
